mnemonics.title: Subgroup Test One-Step
mnemonics.content: I Can Find Inverse Elements Easily (ICFIE): If for all a,b in H, a*b⁻¹ is in H, then H is a subgroup. I-Identity, C-Closure, F-Inverse easy to check.

mnemonics.title: Subgroup Test Two-Step
mnemonics.content: Close Your Eyes And Invert (CYAI): Closure (ab∈H) + Inverses (a⁻¹∈H) = Subgroup.

acronyms.expansion: H is a subgroup of G
acronyms.usage: Standard notation for subgroup relationship, ≤ suggests subset and inherit structure

acronyms.expansion: H is a normal subgroup of G
acronyms.usage: Denotes H is invariant under conjugation by elements of G

acronyms.expansion: Center of group G = {g ∈ G : gx = xg for all x ∈ G}
acronyms.usage: Elements that commute with everything in G

acronyms.expansion: Cyclic subgroup generated by element a = {aⁿ : n ∈ Z}
Index of subgroup H in G = number of cosets
acronyms.usage: Measures 'size' of subgroup relative to whole group

visualTips.visualization: Draw group G as a large circle. Subgroups are smaller circles entirely inside G, with the identity element at the center common to all.
Think of cosets as 'shifts' of the subgroup H by elements of G. Each coset is a copy of H, and these copies tile G without overlap (like wallpaper patterns).
In cyclic group ⟨a⟩, think of a as 'step' around a circle. After n steps (aⁿ) you return to identity e. The order is number of steps to return.
Z(G) is the 'downtown' of the group - elements in the center don't cause 'traffic' (commutation issues) with anyone else.

analogies.analogy: Just as a team within an organization retains the organizational structure, a subgroup retains the group operation within a larger group.
Each coset is like a parallel universe (copy of H) that doesn't overlap with others. Together they cover the entire group G.
A normal subgroup H is like a 'protected club' - when you 'visit' it from outside (conjugation), you always come back to the same club.

analogies.analization: The index [G:H] tells you how many 'magnified copies' of H fit inside G, like how many tiles of size |H| cover area |G|.
